What you can do next

What Is Wage Theft?

When you don't get paid what you earned

Wage theft happens when an employer fails to pay a worker the full wages they're legally owed. It's the most common form of workplace theft in the country — and most workers who experience it don't realize it's illegal.

Unpaid overtime

Working more than 40 hours a week without getting time-and-a-half for the extra hours.

Paid below minimum wage

Getting an hourly rate lower than what your state requires, even if you agreed to it.

Working off the clock

Being asked to prep, clean up, or keep working after you've clocked out, unpaid.

Stolen tips

Your employer keeping part or all of your tips, or pooling them in ways the law doesn't allow.

Missing final paycheck

Not receiving your last paycheck on time — or at all — after you quit or were let go.

Illegal deductions

Money taken out of your pay for uniforms, breakages, or shortages without your consent.

Common myths

Myth

"I agreed to be paid this way, so it's legal."

Fact

Minimum wage and overtime rules can't be waived by agreement.

Myth

"I'm undocumented, so I have no rights."

Fact

Labor laws protect every worker regardless of immigration status.

Myth

"I'm paid salary, so overtime doesn't apply."

Fact

Many salaried workers still legally qualify for overtime pay.

Myth

"It's too late — this happened months ago."

Fact

You generally have up to 3 years to file a claim in VA, MD, and DC.

Think this might be happening to you?

Your rights, explained

Tap a topic to learn more — these apply regardless of immigration status.

Every hour you work must be paid at least the state minimum wage — no exceptions for tips, training, or trial periods.

VA $12.77/hr MD $15.00/hr DC $17.50/hr

If you work more than 40 hours in a week, you're generally owed 1.5× your regular rate for every hour past 40 — even if you're paid a flat salary or "off the books."

It's illegal for your employer to fire, demote, cut your hours, or threaten you for asking about your pay, filing a complaint, or talking to a coworker about wages.

You're entitled to a record of your hours and pay for every pay period. Keeping your own notes — photos of schedules, texts about hours — can help if you ever need to file a claim.

Labor laws protect every worker regardless of immigration status. You have the same right to minimum wage, overtime, and a safe workplace as anyone else. Your immigration status is NOT shared when you file a complaint.

Beyond the wages you're owed, you may be entitled to additional damages and, in some cases, your attorney's fees covered — at no upfront cost to you.

Statute of limitations: You generally have 3 years to file a claim in Virginia, Maryland, and DC. Don't wait too long.

Step-by-step guide

How to Collect Proof

Start documenting now — even if you haven't decided to report yet. Good records make all the difference.

📋

Prove you did the work

📱
Take photos at the job site
Timestamped photos place you there. Capture the site, tools, and your progress.
📓
Keep a daily work log
Write the date, start/end time, location, and tasks each day. Use your phone notes — only you need to see it.
💬
Screenshot every work message
WhatsApp, SMS, email — any message where your boss gives instructions proves you were assigned the work.
🤝
Write down coworker names
People who saw you work can be witnesses. Save their names and numbers now while you still have contact.
📦
Save receipts and delivery records
Sign-out sheets, delivery confirmations, or supply receipts all tie you to specific dates and tasks.
💰

Prove what you were promised

✉️
Get the pay rate in writing first
Before starting any job, ask your boss to confirm the pay by text. Even "I'll pay you $X/hr" is legally useful.
📸
Screenshot the job posting
Job ads get deleted. Save a screenshot the moment you see it — it proves what pay was advertised.
🖊️
Confirm verbal agreements by text
If your boss told you verbally, send a text right after: "Just to confirm, you said you'll pay me $X per hour." Their reply (or silence) is evidence.
🧾
Keep every pay stub and cash envelope
Paid in cash? Keep the envelope. Got a slip? Photograph it before you spend anything. This shows exactly what they paid.
📄
Request your payroll records
You have the legal right to see your pay records in DC, MD, and VA. Ask by text so there is a written record that you requested them.
🎙️
Record your employer confirming the deal (with their consent)
Ask your employer directly: "Can I record this so I remember the details?" Then have them say out loud the pay rate, the number of hours, and the job being done. A recording of your employer saying "I will pay you $X for X hours of [job]" is among the strongest evidence you can have.
⚠️ Always get consent first — recording without permission may be illegal in your state.
💡

You have up to 3 years to file in DC, MD, and VA — but records disappear fast. Start on day one.

Submit a Report

Your information goes directly to free legal advocates who can help. Nothing is shared without your permission.
